content.text: A meeting prep task asks Cowork to gather and synthesize everything a participant needs to walk into a meeting informed: account status, open issues, prior commitments, pending decisions, and a recommended agenda. The engine reads from connected data sources — CRM notes, email threads, Jira tickets, Slack channels, Drive documents — and produces a concise brief tailored to the specific meeting type and audience. The result is what it would take a prepared human 30-60 minutes to assemble, delivered in one task run.
Meeting Prep Task Types

attrs.alt: Meeting prep task types: client meeting, internal strategy, negotiation, and board briefing — each with characteristic sources and output sections

content.text: The goal structure for a meeting prep task varies by meeting type. The diagram shows four common types with their typical source sets and output sections. The common thread: every prep brief is written for a specific attendee with a specific role — that audience definition is what shapes what to include and what depth to go to.
Goal Structure for a Meeting Prep Task
A meeting prep goal has five components: the meeting context, the attendee profile, the sources to query, the output sections to produce, and the output path.
Meeting Context Block
Describe the meeting in enough detail that the engine can assess relevance when reading sources. Name the participants, their organizations, the meeting objective, and any known agenda items.

Meeting: Q3 Business Review with Acme Corp
Date: 2024-07-15
Participants: Sarah Chen (Acme Corp, VP Product), James Okafor (our side, Account Executive)
Objective: Review Q2 performance against targets, discuss renewal terms, address product concerns
Known agenda items: Q2 metrics review, pricing discussion, roadmap preview request
Attendee Profile Block
Name the person who will read the prep brief and their role. This determines what level of background to include, what terminology is appropriate, and what decisions they are likely to be making.

Prep brief for: James Okafor (Account Executive)
James has been on this account for 8 months. He knows the product well but
may not have read recent support tickets or the product concern thread in Slack.
Brief should flag anything he may have missed, not re-explain basics.
Sources Block
List every connector and file the engine should read. Include date ranges and filters to scope retrieval to relevant content.

Sources to query:
- Google Drive (connector: Google Drive): /Clients/Acme-Corp/ folder
  Include: meeting notes, contracts, QBR templates
  Exclude: files older than 2023-01-01

- Jira (connector: Jira): project ACME, all open issues + issues closed in last 90 days
  Filter: priority = High or Critical; OR label = 'acme-concern'

- Slack (connector: Slack): #account-acme channel, last 60 days
  Also check: any DMs between James and Sarah Chen (if accessible)

- /workspace/clients/acme-corp/q2-metrics.csv (uploaded manually)
Output Sections Block
Specify the exact sections, their order, and length constraints. A meeting prep brief is a decision support document — it should be readable in 5 minutes, not 20.

Output sections:
1. Account Status at a Glance (3-5 bullet points: health, ARR, renewal date, NPS)
2. Open Issues and Commitments (table: issue | priority | age | who owns it)
3. What Has Changed Since Last Meeting (bullet list, most recent first)
4. What Acme Is Likely to Raise (3-5 bullets with supporting evidence from sources)
5. Suggested Talking Points (bullet list for James to use during the meeting)
6. Recommended Agenda (ordered list with time allocations)
7. Red Flags (anything urgent that needs addressing before or during the meeting)

Target length: 500-700 words total
Output to /workspace/outputs/acme-qbr-prep-2024-07-15.md
Complete Client Meeting Prep Goal

## Meeting Prep: Acme Corp Q3 Business Review

### Meeting Context
Meeting: Q3 Business Review with Acme Corp, 2024-07-15
Participants: Sarah Chen (Acme Corp, VP Product), James Okafor (AE, our side)
Objective: Q2 metrics review, renewal discussion, address product concerns

### Attendee
Prep brief for James Okafor (Account Executive).
Assume he knows the account history but may have missed recent Slack and Jira activity.

### Sources
- Google Drive /Clients/Acme-Corp/ (modified after 2024-01-01)
- Jira: project ACME, open issues + closed in last 90 days (High/Critical priority)
- Slack #account-acme (last 60 days)
- /workspace/clients/acme-corp/q2-metrics.csv

### Output
1. Account Status at a Glance (5 bullets max)
2. Open Issues and Commitments (table: issue | priority | age | owner)
3. What Changed Since Last QBR (bullet list)
4. What Acme Is Likely to Raise (3-5 bullets with source citations)
5. Suggested Talking Points for James
6. Recommended Agenda (with time allocations)
7. Red Flags

Length: 500-700 words. Output to /workspace/outputs/acme-qbr-prep-2024-07-15.md
Internal Meeting Prep

Internal meeting prep differs from client prep in scope and tone. The brief is for an internal participant who may need to align with colleagues, present a position, or make a decision. The engine reads internal sources (Slack, project docs, Jira roadmap) and synthesizes what is still open, where there is disagreement, and what the meeting needs to resolve.

## Meeting Prep: Quarterly Roadmap Review

### Meeting Context
Meeting: Q3 Roadmap Review (internal), 2024-07-18
Participants: product, engineering leads, design (all internal)
Objective: finalize Q3 priorities, resolve backlog disputes, sign off on engineering capacity

### Attendee
Prep for: Alex Petrov (Head of Product)
Alex needs to go in with a clear view of which items are disputed and why,
what the engineering team flagged as at-risk, and what decisions are needed.

### Sources
- Slack #roadmap-q3 (last 45 days)
- Jira: ROADMAP project, Q3 epics and stories (all statuses)
- Google Drive /Product/Roadmap/ (modified after 2024-05-01)
- /workspace/inputs/q3-capacity-estimate.md (uploaded by engineering lead)

### Output
1. Items Finalized (no further discussion needed)
2. Items Disputed (table: item | disagreement | stakeholders | what would resolve it)
3. Items at Risk (engineering flagged concerns)
4. Decisions Needed in This Meeting (ordered list)
5. Recommended Agenda
6. Pre-Read Links (Drive documents Alex should skim before the meeting)

Length: 400-600 words. Output to /workspace/outputs/q3-roadmap-prep-2024-07-18.md
Board and Executive Briefing Prep

Board and executive briefings require a different register: data-forward, assumption-explicit, and written for an audience that is not embedded in day-to-day context. The prep brief tells the presenter what the audience is likely to focus on and what they will need explained.
What to Include in Board Prep
Section
Content
Why It Helps
Key metrics
Revenue, growth rate, burn, headcount — current vs target vs prior period
Board members read these first; getting them wrong damages credibility
Decisions needed
Explicit list of what the board needs to approve or vote on
Prevents ambiguity about what the meeting is for
Likely questions
Based on prior meeting notes and board member known interests
Helps presenter prepare answers in advance
Risks to flag proactively
Issues the presenter should surface before being asked
Builds trust; surprises damage it
Context for non-technical members
Plain-language explanation of technical topics on the agenda
Board often includes non-technical members unfamiliar with product specifics

## Board Briefing Prep: July 2024 Board Meeting

### Meeting Context
Board meeting, 2024-07-22. Presenting: CEO.
Agenda: Q2 financial results, product milestone update, Series B timing discussion.

### Attendee
Prep for CEO. She needs to know what questions to expect, what data to have ready,
and where the board is likely to probe. Assume she knows the business; skip basics.

### Sources
- Google Drive /Finance/Q2-Results/ (all files)
- Google Drive /Board/Prior-Meetings/ (last 2 board decks)
- Jira: product milestone epics (Q2 delivery status)
- /workspace/inputs/q2-board-deck-draft.pdf

### Output
1. Q2 Metrics Snapshot (revenue, ARR, burn, headcount vs plan)
2. Likely Board Questions (bullet list with suggested answers)
3. Decisions Required (explicit list)
4. Risks to Flag Proactively
5. What Changed Since Last Board Meeting (bullet list)
6. Talking Points for Series B Timing Discussion

Length: 700-1000 words. Output to /workspace/outputs/board-prep-2024-07-22.md
Scheduling Recurring Meeting Prep

For recurring meetings (weekly 1:1s, monthly client check-ins, quarterly board meetings), use a Cowork scheduled task to generate the prep brief automatically before each meeting. The goal stays constant; the data changes each cycle.

# Scheduled task: weekly 1:1 prep (runs every Monday at 7 AM)

Generate a meeting prep brief for my weekly 1:1 with direct reports.

Sources:
- Jira: issues assigned to each direct report (updated or created in last 7 days)
- Slack: any message in #team-updates from the last 7 days
- /workspace/1on1s/open-items.md (maintained manually)

Output one section per direct report:
- Their name and current sprint focus
- Open items from last week (from /workspace/1on1s/open-items.md)
- Any blockers or concerns surfaced in Jira or Slack
- Suggested discussion topics

Output to /workspace/outputs/1on1-prep-{YYYY-MM-DD}.md
Length: 150-200 words per person.
Common Pitfalls in Meeting Prep Goals
Pitfall
Symptom
Fix
Goal too broad
Brief contains everything; no clear priorities
Name the meeting objective explicitly; tell the engine to rank by relevance to that objective
Audience not specified
Brief explains things the reader already knows; omits what they actually need
Always name the attendee and their familiarity level with the account or topic
No output structure defined
Engine invents its own section order; brief does not match your workflow
List sections explicitly with length constraints
Sources too wide
Brief is long but thin; too many sources dilute depth
Prioritize 2-3 high-signal sources; add others only if key information is missing
No recency filter
Brief includes information from 2 years ago that is no longer relevant
Always include a date range filter on connector calls
Generic talking points
Talking points could apply to any meeting
Ask the engine to tie each talking point to a specific source claim (e.g. cite the Slack message or Jira ticket)
Post-Meeting Follow-up as a Paired Task

Meeting prep and post-meeting follow-up work best as paired tasks. The prep brief sets context; the follow-up task uses the meeting output (notes, decisions, action items) to produce a structured summary and update the workspace for the next cycle.
Post-Meeting Follow-up Goal

## Post-Meeting Follow-up: Acme Corp QBR 2024-07-15

Read /workspace/inputs/meeting-notes-2024-07-15.md (uploaded meeting notes).

Produce:
1. Decisions Made (bullet list with decision and who made it)
2. Action Items (table: item | owner | due date | source quote from notes)
3. Open Questions (items raised but not resolved)
4. Commitments We Made to Acme (what we promised; flag anything that needs follow-up)
5. What Changed Since Prep Brief (/workspace/outputs/acme-qbr-prep-2024-07-15.md):
   - Red flags that were addressed vs still open
   - New issues raised that were not in the prep brief

Update /workspace/1on1s/open-items.md: append action items from this meeting.
Output full follow-up to /workspace/outputs/acme-qbr-followup-2024-07-15.md
Building a Meeting History File

For recurring client or stakeholder meetings, maintain a meeting history file in /workspace/ that accumulates decisions and action items across all sessions. The next prep brief reads this file to produce the 'open commitments' section without re-querying the same connectors.

# Append to every post-meeting follow-up goal:
Append a dated entry to /workspace/clients/acme-corp/meeting-history.md:

---
## 2024-07-15 QBR
Decisions: [from decisions made section]
Commitments made: [from commitments section]
Open from this meeting: [from open questions section]
---

# The next prep brief includes:
Read /workspace/clients/acme-corp/meeting-history.md.
In the 'Open Issues and Commitments' section, include any
commitments from prior meetings that have not been marked resolved.
Sourcing Hierarchy for Meeting Prep
Not all sources are equally reliable for meeting prep. The engine should prioritize sources in a defined order so that authoritative recent data is not mixed equally with old or informal content.
Source Tier
Examples
When to Use
Trust Level
Tier 1 — Authoritative
Signed contracts, official reports, CRM account records
Account status, revenue, contractual commitments
High — cite directly
Tier 2 — Curated
Meeting notes in Drive, QBR decks, project plans
Historical context, prior decisions
High — cite with date
Tier 3 — Conversational
Slack threads, email chains
Recent sentiment, informal commitments, emerging issues
Medium — note informality
Tier 4 — Transactional
Jira tickets, support logs
Open issues, bug reports, feature requests
High for factual status, low for interpretation
Include the tier hierarchy in the synthesis rules block of your goal so the engine weights sources appropriately when they conflict:

# Synthesis rules (include in goal):
When sources conflict:
- Prefer Tier 1 (contracts, CRM records) over Tier 3 (Slack) on account status
- Prefer Tier 3 (Slack, email) over Tier 2 (prior meeting notes) on recent sentiment
- Cite Tier 3 sources as 'informal' or 'reported in Slack'
- Never present a Slack opinion as confirmed fact
Customizing Prep for Different Meeting Roles

The same meeting may require different prep briefs for different attendees. An account executive needs client history and talking points; a product manager attending the same meeting needs feature request history and roadmap positioning. Write separate goals for each attendee role rather than one generic brief.

# Two goals for the same meeting:

# Goal 1 — prep for Account Executive James Okafor:
Focus on: account health, renewal status, open support issues, relationship dynamics
Tone: relationship-oriented
Sections: account status, open issues, suggested talking points, red flags

# Goal 2 — prep for Product Manager Priya Sharma:
Focus on: feature requests from Acme, roadmap items Acme has expressed interest in,
product concerns mentioned in Jira and Slack
Tone: product-oriented
Sections: feature requests (by priority), roadmap items Acme cares about,
product concerns flagged, what to avoid committing to without eng sign-off
One brief per attendee role is more useful than one comprehensive brief for all. Comprehensiveness increases length; length decreases the chance any attendee reads it fully.
Summary
A meeting prep task collects from connected sources (CRM, Jira, Slack, Drive) and produces a brief tailored to a specific meeting type and attendee.
Every goal needs five components: meeting context, attendee profile, sources, output sections, and an output path.
Name the attendee and their familiarity level — this determines what to include (gaps in their knowledge) and what to omit (what they already know).
Use explicit output sections with length constraints to prevent the engine from producing an unfocused dump.
For recurring meetings, schedule the prep task to run automatically before each cycle so the brief is ready without manual triggering.
Tie talking points and recommendations to specific source evidence — vague talking points are not useful in a meeting.
Keep total brief length under 1000 words for most meetings; brevity means the recipient actually reads it before walking in.
